findings.description: Executive Summary's Top 5 Priorities lists REC-RSK-008 (umbrella) at ~$25.0M and REC-RSK-004 (ILIT/SUL) at ~$10.0M, but ranks them #1 and #2 by 'estimated impact'. These figures are coverage face amounts, not estimated impact (savings or value transferred). REC-RSK-007 (~$2.0M) is also a face amount. Mixing face-amount notional with the impact-dollar convention used elsewhere in the plan (e.g., REC-EST-005 at ~$6.0M of estate tax savings) is misleading — the umbrella's $25M is not comparable to a $6M estate-tax savings figure.
findings.evidence: {rank:1, REC-RSK-008 (Risk & Insurance), ~$25.0M, 0-30 days}, {rank:2, REC-RSK-004 (Risk & Insurance), ~$10.0M}, ... {rank:3, REC-EST-005 (Estate), ~$6.0M}

findings.description: RB.3 closer states 'PTET savings alone fund the entire annual cost of the buy/sell life insurance program in Section 5 with room to spare.' RB.5 closer states the aggregate annual premium load runs '$30K-$60K, which is approximately what the PTET election in Section 3 saves on its own.' PTET is quantified at ~$73K/yr. $30K-$60K vs $73K is consistent with 'with room to spare,' but RB.5 frames PTET savings and premium as 'approximately' equal — which understates the cushion. Minor cross-section framing inconsistency the advisor should reconcile.
findings.evidence: RB.3: 'The PTET savings alone fund the entire annual cost of the buy/sell life insurance program in Section 5 with room to spare.' RB.5: 'The aggregate annual premium load across the full business-side insurance program runs roughly $30K-$60K, which is approximately what the PTET election in Section 3 saves on its own.'

findings.description: Decisions Needed surfaces REC-RSK-011 (standalone LTC) and REC-RSK-012 (hybrid LTC) as a mutual-exclusion tie still pending sign-off, but RP.9 Section 9C presents the hybrid LTC decision as already made ('Choose hybrid LTC life over standalone LTC. We compared standalone LTC against hybrid LTC-life across a 30-year horizon. Hybrid wins...'). Either DN should reflect that the recommendation is firm pending only client confirmation, or RP.9 should present it as a comparison awaiting decision. As written, the two sections contradict each other on the decision status.
findings.evidence: RP.9: 'Choose hybrid LTC life over standalone LTC. We compared standalone LTC against hybrid LTC-life across a 30-year horizon. Hybrid wins for an HNW couple...' vs DN row 4: 'REC-RSK-012: decision pending ... decisions_needed=true reflects mutual-exclusion tie with REC-RSK-011 pending final client/advisor sign-off.'

findings.description: RB.4 cites annual exclusion gifting capacity as '4 donees × 2 spouses × $19K = $152K/year' producing '$3.04M of estate removal' over 20 years. RP.12 12B repeats the math but the underlying multiplication 4 × 2 × $19K = $152K is correct, while $152K × 20yr = $3.04M is also correct. However, RP.12 12B then says '4 donees (Olivia, James, Sophie, plus a 4th donee added through Crummey-noticed irrevocable trust mechanics) × 2 spouses × $19K = $152K per year' — introducing a 4th donee that is not present in RB.4. The donee count is internally consistent at 4 but the identity of the 4th donee differs between sections (RB.4 implies 4 children-equivalent; RP.12 explicitly invents a 'Crummey-noticed irrevocable trust' as the 4th donee). Advisor should reconcile.
findings.evidence: RB.4: '$152K/year of capacity for the Holloways: 4 donees × 2 spouses × $19K.' RP.12: '4 donees (Olivia, James, Sophie, plus a 4th donee added through Crummey-noticed irrevocable trust mechanics) × 2 spouses × $19K = $152K per year.'

findings.description: FO Retirement opportunity cites '$8,600/year of tax-free growth capacity' from clearing Catherine's pro-rata block via 401(k) rollover. RP.10 addresses this ('doubling annual capacity to $17,200'). Coverage is present but the figure $8,600 in FO maps to Marcus's solo capacity, while the unblock-Catherine number is also $8,600 — text could read as if both numbers describe the same item. Minor; advisor should verify the FO opportunity reads cleanly against the RP.10 treatment.
findings.evidence: FO: 'a workplace-401(k) rollover would unlock another $8,600/year of tax-free growth capacity.' RP.10: 'Marcus has clean backdoor Roth capacity at $8,600 per year ... doubling annual capacity to $17,200.'

findings.description: RB.4 and RP.12 both quantify the same estate-planning layers (children's trusts $4M-$8M, GRAT ~$4.2M, IDGT $8M-$15M, SLAT $5.4M-$16.2M, GST $50M-$500M). RP.12's combined-estate closer states the sum 'is on the order of $20M+ at the midpoint,' which roughly aligns with the cited layers. However, RB.4 closer claims the layered savings 'eliminate the bulk of the projected $11.4M-$17.6M federal estate tax exposure' — a much smaller framing than RP.12's $20M+ midpoint. Both can be true depending on framing (estate-tax dollars saved vs. value transferred outside estate), but the narrative connective is unclear and could confuse the reader.
findings.evidence: RB.4 closer: 'eliminate the bulk of the projected $11.4M-$17.6M federal estate tax exposure'; RP.12 closer: 'cumulative estate-tax savings range across the layers is on the order of $20M+ at the midpoint'
